title: Ten Maps of Greenland
description:
Greenland has been in the headlines everywhere due to the political tensions. But beyond the controversy, this island, roughly the size of Mexico, has many unique features. Here are ten maps highlighting some of the many curiosities of this European territory perched atop the American continent.
Greenland is home to the U.S. Pituffik Space Base, known formerly as Thule Air Base is the primary active U.S. military installation in Greenland, located in the northwest for missile warning and space surveillance. Following WWII and Cold War expansion, the U.S. abandoned dozens of sites, including the Camp Century ice-covered nuclear site. Denmark also currently have bases in Greenland, the Joint Arctic Command operates in Greenland's capital, Nuuk, focusing on surveillance and search-and-rescue.
Here is a map showing the current bases and some others that the USA maintained in the past.

body_complment.text_wide: Most of Greenland is covered by a massive ice sheet in the middle of the island. That ice blanket is about 3,5km thick at its maximum, and by area of extent is the second largest body of ice in the world, only behind Antarctica. Greenland’s ice sheet alone is larger than the combined areas of France, Germany, and Spain. Here’s a map of Greenland’s ice sheet and glaciers based on data from Enveo Cryoportal.

The ice in Greenland is melting at an incredible rate, twice as fast as in Antarctica. NASA has been monitoring the change in Greenland's ice mass since 2002, and the trend shows a decrease of 266 billion metric tons of ice per year, enough to fill 100 million Olympic-sized swimming pools. Greenland’s outer glaciers are experiencing rapid acceleration, thinning, and increased calving, significantly contributing to sea-level rise. Researchers estimate a rise in sea level of about 7m if the ice sheet melts. Key, fast-moving glaciers include Jakobshavn Isbræ, Kangerdlugssuaq and Helheim, and Nioghalvfjerdsbræ the 79°N Glacier, which collectively drain a large percentage of the ice sheet.

Below you can see a map showing the magnitude of ice velocity in Greenland derived by the European Space Agency using SAR data from Sentinel-1 acquired between 2019 and 2020.

That, of course, makes me wonder what Greenland would look like without that enormous ice sheet. GEBCO offers global bathymetry data, and when I downloaded the sub-ice data for the area of Greenland I found that most of the interior of the island is under the sea level. Without the ice connecting the land, Greenland would look like a cluster of large islands rather than a single landmass, with jagged coastlines and deep fjords. Below you can see a map of Greenland with no ice.

Having the middle of the territory completely covered by a shell of ice has, of course, determined where its inhabitants have settled. Greenland’s population is about 57,000(as of 2026), most of the people lives in coastal, ice-free areas. The capital, Nuuk, is home to nearly 20,000 of Greenlanders, but the island is full of tiny settlements of just a few residents as you can see in the map below. (data from OSM)

And talking about Greenland’s residents, polar bears are an icon for greenlanders. Greenland's coat of arms features a polar bear. For Greenlanders, the polar bear —or nanoq— more than an animal or a symbol. They are a central figure in Inuit mythology, representing the strength and resilience needed to survive in the Arctic. Polar bears in Greenland are in the low thousands, primarily inhabit the remote eastern and northern coastal regions, relying on sea ice for hunting seals.

A group of scientists from the University of Washington and the National Snow Ice and Data Center, followed bears in Southeast Greenland for seven years and after a genetic analysis they discovered that polar bears in the south are a distinct subpopulation that has adapted to survive with less sea ice. Below is a map showing their habitad areas based in info provided by NASA's Earth Observatory.

Greenland holds significant untapped mineral wealth, particularly rare earth elements that are really appealing for the global tech industry. The island seats on top of major deposits of iron ore, zinc, copper, gold, graphite, uranium, titanium, and vanadium, with potential for oil and gas offshore. While exploration reveals vast potential, especially in southern and western areas, challenges include limited exploration, harsh climate, environmental concerns, like uranium mining bans, and complex political factors hold investment. Here's a set of mineral deposits identified by the Geological Survey of Denmark and Greenland.

A map of Greenland (or ten) represents much more than just an ice-covered shape. Maps can distort perception, simplify complexity, or transform the unknown into memorable stories. Behind these ten maps lies a community of Greenlanders with much more to tell. Decisions about their resources and development should not rest with distant observers. Those decisions will ultimately transform their lives. I personally believe that everyone has the right to tell their own story; the right to draw their own maps.

title: The dimming of fireflies
description: Once common nighttime sightings, fireflies are now vanishing. Scientists warn their decline is driven by habitat loss, pesticides, and light pollution disrupting their glowing courtship. Without action, future generations may never see their bioluminous dance.

body_complment.text_wide:
As a child in Costa Rica, I remember seeing the cow pastures filled with dancing lights. At night, my town was very dark, and on a moonless night, on a nearby creek, you could clearly see the edge of the Milky Way merging with the lights of the fireflies dancing all around you… magic.
It doesn’t look like that anymore, and not just in my town, but all over the world.

Light pollution is one of the factors contributing to the decline of their populations, as it interferes with their communication systems. This, in addition to the fact that as sea levels rise, their habitats are permanently flooded, leading to the loss of conventional breeding grounds. Both of these causalities are highly pronounced in North America where over the past 20 years, cities become more brightly lit.
Native to North America (1) and Asia (2 and 3), these are some of the species that are on the verge of extinction.

This is part of a series of pages I worked on last year for the German magazine INGRAPHICS. The 85-pages magazine is dedicated to data vizualization, if you want to see all the maps I created for the magazine and many more great graphics from authors all over the world, you can get a copy here: https://schwochow.shop/products/ingraphics-magazine

title: Snowy Christmas 2025
description: Contrary to popular belief, it’s not that common to see snow in New York City in November, or at least not in recent years. This year we had snowflakes very early on and a couple of full snowfalls well into December. Two years ago we also had reports of possible snow in November, and that’s why I decided to create (these animations showing the dance of snow) at the North Pole back then.

body_complment.text_wide: This year, I used the same data and created new maps; as before, the data comes from the US National Ice Center (usicecenter.gov) Below you can see the picture of the snow and ice at the North Pole this Christmas.
But perhaps the fun part is watching the almost two-month-long dance of the snow, seeing how the snow spreads across the continent is fun, don’t you think?
Or even if it's just ice, here's the same time frame looking at ice only.

It’s incredible to see the dynamics of our planet. I was born near the equator, so seasons aren’t the same thing. You might see the change by the amount of rain only. That's one of my favorite things of New York, the city fully transforms into something else with each season. Here's the contrast in ice and snow between Nov. 01 and Dec. 28.
Happy Hollidays, see you again in 2026!

title: How helicopters sketch-out Manhattan
description:
New York City is always bustling with traffic, it doesn’t matter if you’re talking about its streets or its skies. There are so many daily flights of all kinds of aircraft that you can see the lines they leave behind trace the outline of the Hudson River to the west of Manhattan, or the East River in the other side of the island as well.
In the image below, you can see purple dots representing air traffic in Arbil 9, 2025.
Observe the straight lines left by airplanes approaching airports and how these lines converge at crossroads as they land or take off.
On the other hand, the most organic patterns are created by helicopters flying low over the rivers; there are some loops near the Statue of Liberty, there are even some circles here and there, those are probably police helicopters conducting surveillance or belonging to a TV channel.

body_complment.text_wide:
This phenomenon isn’t unique to this city, but NYC is a place where the intense air traffic creates this distinctive pattern. I live just across the East River in Astoria, some mornings helicopters doing loops have woken me up at 4am, they are like those mosquitoes that buzz around your ears when you are camping outdoors.

But if you live in NYC, even though helicopters are annoying, you get used to them. Below is a close-up showing traffic over Manhattan and its surroundings in different days of April 2025.

As you might noticed, suddenly, one day everything went a little more quiet on the river banks. On April 10, 2025, a Bell 206 helicopter on a sightseeing tour crashed into the Hudson River. All six onboard—a family of five Spanish tourist and the pilot—died. The skies were paralyzed for a few days, and just for one moment, the shape of the island was not drawn by aircraft as usual.
That event took me to explore these curious patterns in the air traffic. Check below a few maps looking at the skies of those days but only looking at helicopters flying between April 9 - 15.

After looking at that sequence, is kind of shocking to see how the skies returned to normal in just a few days. By April 15, the usual patterns were back again, by density of paths, the 14th looks almost with even more activity than the days before the accident. Here’s a larger map of the following day showing all traffic once more with helicopters highlighted in light brown and other aircraft in purple.

It’s a very busy city, and the sky is no exception. This is just a curiosity about how air traffic patterns can permanently draw shapes in the sky; it seemed to me that there was a story behind the patterns that could be briefly told. The accident that day was tragic; the city has many regulations for drones and helicopters, but even so, an accident can happen anytime, anywhere.

–Thanks for reading!

title: Myanmar political violence
description: Myanmar is home to about 57 million. The British ruled the country (Burma) since 1820 and gained full independence in 1948, but since then civil wars have asphyxiated the country mostly due ethical conflicts and the struggle to balance power and the militias.

body_complment.text_wide: The map above visualizes reports of political violence in Myanmar this year from both, state forces and political militias clustered in a 10km-grid. The details below show areas with high activity, red for state forces and blue for militias.

Since 2010, ACLED has been documenting political violence in Myanmar; you can find out more about their reports and how they gather information about this country and other conflicts on their website.

title: The Afar Triangle
description: This year I was thinking to join the #30daymapchallenge. But time and procastination forced me to do brief appearances as a plan-B 😅. The prompt for November 5th is ’earth’, so, here’s a map of the Afar Triangle.

body_complment.text_wide: Since I learned about this place, it easily captured my attention, the Afar Triangle is a depression in the great rift valley in eastern Africa. It has a triple junction of plates pulling in different directions, and a bunch more of curiosities. It's northern region is below 100m under the sea level, it's also the hottest place on Earth, (more then Death Valley yes) and it has hypersaline hot lakes.

I love to see the many geological features clashing into this place, many volcanic formations, plates pulling at different speeds and directions, rivers that evaporate, mountains and lakes everywhere... But tuns out that not all, the region is also home of the oldest human ancestor, Lucy who died and probably lived its life in the Afar Triangle some 3 million years ago. Here you have 2 more close-up details of the main map.

I'll try to post a few more maps to join the #30daymapchallenge of this year. I wish I can have a clone of my self to do more stuff!
–See you soon (hopefully).

title: Happy Birthday Costa Rica!
description:
This year Costa Rica turns 204 years of independence. My country is a little piece of land of about 51,100 square kilometers. However the seas sorounding it, make it a bit larger, about 589,600 square kilometers of maritime territory. That's about 11.5 times the size of the land area.
While browsing around, I came across this map showing tectonic plates and elevation profiles, and I thought it would be fun to make a 'terraced' version... so, here it is.

body_complment.text_wide: There has always been a great appreciation for nature conservation in Costa Rica, in fact, the country expanded the protected areas around the Cocos Island a few years ago. The move aims to protect this crucial corridor for many species in this region of the Pacific Ocean.

If you're curious about the source of the information, I got the contour profiles from Axismaps, and the rest is just Natural Earth and the original map I described above. I used QGIS to generate the base map, Blender to render the terrain and Adibe Illustrator for labels. Thanks for visiting my blog, and happy birthday, Costa Rica!.

title: The Kerguelen Islands
description:
If you're ever looking to escape from someone, this place could be a good option. This archipelago is one of the most isolated places in the world; the nearest inhabited place is the island of Madagascar, in the eastern side of the African coast, but it's located more than 3,300 km away.
These islands are French territory and their motto is 'Liberty, equality, fraternity.' It's great to know that their inhabitants are fraternal because once you arrive there, it's not easy to leave.

body_complment.text_wide: According to Wikipedia, the islands were officially discovered by the French navigator Yves-Joseph de Kerguelen-Trémarec on 12 February 1772. The next day, Charles de Boisguehenneuc landed and claimed the island for the French crown. Here you have a couple of enlarged areas of this peculiar place.

title: The Cityverse
description:
Like soap bubbles, the cities we live in are like micro-universes, self-contained units that somehow gather everything they need to function. I love how each of these mini-universes has its own identity. Every place I've been to has something unique, a particular way of doing things so to speak.
For this reason, I was thinking about visualizing different cities, some because I have them tattooed on my soul, others because they seem curious to me and they contrast with each other. To do this I have used data from Copernicus Global Human Settlement Layer (GHSL) and Sentinel's Land use classification.Below you can see my hometown in its micro universe.
The brown markings are buildings in the central valley. The darker the markings, the taller the buildings according to the GHSL classification.

body_complment.text_wide: This particular city seems to have a lot of empty space, but the wide mountain ranges on the sides have shaped the city's expansion into this curious pattern, but there are also other things that make it unique, such as the amount of vegetation around it. See what the same place looks like by adding a layer of vegetation from Sentiel land use.

Following the same principle, I have applied the same process to create bubbles of a dozen cities. All with their own notable characteristics. Look below and think about the conclusions you can get from these spheres. For example, look at how little green area there is, with a few exceptions. Note that all the spheres you see here are at the same scale of about 80 km2, same as the first two you already saw above.
Denser cities, with large built-up areas look a bit darker, in some cases the water and bare land make for an interesting contrast... all fun to see.

Although it is perhaps a little easier to see the contrast of the extension of the built areas and the heights of those blocks with a version like the first one you saw at the beginning of this post.
In my creative processes I always make many versions of the same thing, regardless of whether it is for my work, or as in this case, just for fun. Here they are again in that other format.

As I said above, some of these places are quite special for me. I spend many years living in Hong Kong, the geography of the place is super interesting and the mix of urban and natural landscapes is just amazing. I think you can see the image in the little tumbnails above, but I think its worth seeing it a little larger, so, if you are looking at this entry on a large screen, here you go:
Not a difference if you are reading from a phone screen tho. Sorry... :)
But let's get back to the nerdy stuff.

There's something about renders that fascinates me, creating pieces in general excites me, but when I see the renders come to life... ugh, I think I'm enjoying this way too much! Here are some screenshots of what the radiance calculations that Cinema 4D does when you use Global Illumination look like.

Speaking of processes, I aligned these pieces using QGIS to process the data, then I made a couple of rasters that I took to Cinema 4D and finally some labels in Adobe Illustrator. Below you have a render halfway through the process.

Of all the dozens of versions I've made and the 10 gigabytes of data I've used, there are a couple that I really love because of the patterns they follow. In some cases, the tallest buildings follow specific streets or financial districts, almost all of them have something like that, here there are some versions without land-use, just buildings without anything else.
Or these other sets where the water flows over the city. Cool patterns I think can be accentuated with a little tweak in color.
Here is a collection of those alternative versions in highg contrast reds and light blue waters:
So, tell me, is your city here? In total I made about 80 renders for this handful of cities, maybe in the future I'll try something different with more... who knows where my obsession will take me.

title: Air allies
description:
Last week was a busy week, with the Los Angeles fires ravaging multiple locations, leaving behind a trail of death and property damage. Our team at the Times has reconstructed the events that occurred during the first 24 hours, which you can read it for free at this link. However, this is just a fraction of all the things we produced. Some things like the map below about the drone incident that caused planes battling the fires to be grounded were left out of coverage for editing reasons.
Since I had all these maps almost ready, I decided to reformulate them to bring them to this space.

body_complment.text_wide: However, although it was an exhausting week for me, it does not compare to the intensity experienced by the firefighters and many other volunteers and officers in California. Just seeing the activity of the air support is incredible. Below some maps with data provided by flightradar24.com
And of course, some more maps from the same package visualizing aerial activity in the same style as the piece published by the New York Times.

title: The land of Brits' disappointment
description:
While surfing the web, I stumbled with this place called Disappointment Island. At first glance, I thought wasn't real, but actually is a thing you can put type and find in google maps!
This tiny human-uninhabited island in the subantarctic Auckland Islands of New Zealand was just perfect for a hand-drawn map, I thought immediately.

body_complment.text_wide: If the images that came out of google are accurate, this place is not dissapointing at all, or at least not in the summer. Accoringly to Wiki, on 1907, the Dundoland shipwrecked near the west side of the island and the surviving crew was stranded there for seven months. However, its european name, ´Disappointment´ Wiki says, was given to it by another british sailor who was there conducting a survey of fur seals. –Spoiler– He didn't find any, so He named the island after the results of his expedition. –Dissapointing Island.

title: Footprints of damage
description: This is more of an infofail than a fun map. In late 2024, I was doing research for a tornado piece I published in December, but some of the information we didn't use included building footprints from other extreme events that occurred during the year. You can read more about this fail on my graphics blog here.

body_complment.text_wide: These maps show clusters of buildings damaged by hurricanes Milton and Helene in 2024. Each red box is a 5 km cluster, the higher and more saturated the color, the more damaged buildings. Learn more about this infofail here.

title: Selenge Delta, Republic of Buryatia
description: The Selenge/Selenga River Delta, is one of the largest inland freshwater floodplains in the world. There are amazing satellite images of this place, not much SAR data tho or at least not that I know of. The resolution of the data on these areas is actually low from Open Source data, and that's why I picked up this place to try a Voxcel Theme on it.

body_complment.text_wide: I think games and art are an awesome source of inspitarion for these type of maps. Not sure if I'll be ever able to publish for real a thing like this, but who knows... maybe I should give a try.

title: Bali, Indonesia
description: The paradisiac Bali. In terms of the island's economy, tourism is the most important. Bali is one of the richest regions in Indonesia. For the past 20 or 30 years, the island has been turning to tourism, according to media such as Time Magazine.

body_complment.text_wide:
However, tourists have brought some development to the island, but as with other natural paradises, this comes at a price.

A few years ago I spent a few days between Denpasar and Nusa Dua, and I can attest to how wonderful this place is. Perhaps those of us who visit these natural paradises should be more aware of what our days of joy can represent for the locals.

title: Lantau, Hong Kong
description: Some of the best years of my life were spent on this island. A combination of spectacular views from the many trails that run in every direction, wonderful beaches, beautiful cobbled squares, temples and so many other wonders. What a great few days we had on Lantau.

title: Singapore
description: A few years ago, while working for Reuters, I lived for a few years on the warm and futuristic island of Singapore. I have very fond memories of that time: long walks along Marina Bay, the Gardens by the Bay, that place on Dempsey Hill where I used to take my family all the time, relaxing in the pool in my place in Clementi, or visiting Westgate and Jem, even the airport...

body_complment.text_wide: To my friends there, I hope you all are doing great, living the good life. You are always in my thoughts.

title: Snowy Christmas
description: I wrote a post at the end of 2023 while seeing a few snowflakes in NYC. On 2022 we barely saw snow in New York, so I was crossing fingers for a rare chance of a white Christmas. I collected data from the US National Ice Center usicecenter.gov to make the map below showing the extent of the snow. The purple spots are population density from the SEDAC.

body_complment.text_wide: There was no snow in NYC on Christmas, but I had a great time with my family tho.

title: Santa María de Dota
description: Santa María de Dota, mi pueblo natal, es un distrito de la provincia de San José, Costa Rica. Dota en general es conocida por su café y su clima fresco. He hecho varios mapas de mi pueblo por diversión, y este es uno de ellos. He añadido un par de detalles como mi casa resaltada sin ningún motivo, pero también recordar el lodo y los ríos que atraviesan el pueblo me traen muchos recuerdos de mis años en estos valles que se elevan a más de dos mil metros sobre el nivel del mar.

body_complment.text_wide: Crecí entre cafetales, montañas y ríos. Y aunque ya hace muchos años que no vivo en Dota, siempre será mi hogar. Qué mejor manera de celebrar mis raíces que con un mapa de mi pueblo.

title: Endeavour
description: En Febrero del 2000, el sistema de radar abordo del transbordador Endeavour capturó datos para crear un modelo digital de las masas continentales de nuestro planeta. Los sets de datos se pusieron al alcance del publico en general 14 años después por orden de La Casa Blanca, aun hoy el SRTM-30 es una de las colecciones de información global más detalladas de libre acceso.

body_complment.text_wide: Miles de personas las hemos usado por años para diversas aplicaciones. Lea más sobre el SRTM aquí..
